start.scenario: It's 45°F, light rain, no shelter, no fire-starting gear. You have 90 minutes of daylight. What do you do first?

options.text: Search for natural shelter (cave, rock overhang)
Gather dry tinder and look for ways to make fire

debris_hut.scenario: Good. You start building. You find enough dry leaves and branches. But the ground is wet. Do you:

options.text: Build a raised debris platform to sleep on
Sleep directly on the leaf pile

natural_search.scenario: You spend 30 minutes searching and find a shallow rock overhang. Not deep enough to fully block wind. Do you:

options.text: Use it as a windbreak and pile debris around yourself

fire_first.scenario: Without fire-starting gear this is risky. 45 minutes pass with no fire. Daylight is almost gone. You now have no shelter and no fire. This was the wrong priority.
fire_first.explanation: In wet, cold conditions without fire gear, shelter is always the first priority. Hypothermia kills faster than hunger or thirst. Fire is secondary when you have no reliable way to start one.

good_outcome.explanation: Correct. Ground insulation is critical — cold ground pulls heat from your body faster than cold air. A raised platform or thick debris layer between you and the ground is essential. You survive the night.

bad_outcome_cold.explanation: Ground conduction would pull heat from your body all night. Even with debris above you, sleeping directly on wet ground leads to hypothermia. Always insulate from below first.

bad_outcome_dark.explanation: The overhang was adequate. Continuing to search costs time and energy — you run out of daylight with no shelter at all. The first viable option is almost always the right option.

scenario: You're building a debris hut for a 40°F night. Which single layer of your debris hut is most critical for preventing hypothermia?

options.text: A thick roof layer to block rain (minimum 2 feet of debris)
The debris packed on the floor beneath you — minimum 6 inches of dry material
A leaf-stuffed door plug to block wind at the entrance
Leaves piled on the outside walls to a thickness of 18 inches

explanation: Ground conduction is the #1 heat thief in shelter scenarios — the earth will pull body heat away 25x faster than cold air of the same temperature. Six inches minimum of dry debris BELOW you is non-negotiable. Roof thickness matters for insulation and rain but is secondary to floor insulation. The door plug prevents wind intrusion but convective loss is less severe than conductive. A good rule: build your floor first, thick enough that you cannot feel the ground through it, then build the roof. If you can only do one, do the floor.
